aliases: Golden egg fried rice with seafood

english: Golden egg fried rice with seafood

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific seafood golden fried rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GKM covers fried rice as a category; no seafood-specific golden fried rice code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for seafood golden fried rice. No suitable parent concept identified for this prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of seafood golden fried rice is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a single plate as typically served at a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Egg and Mixed Seafood — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists fried rice with egg and seafood as the closest matching entry. Values are adapted from this entry. The 'golden' technique (coating rice in egg yolk before stir-frying) adds marginally higher fat and egg content; values are adjusted upward by approximately 5–8% for fat and protein relative to the base HPB entry to reflect the egg-yolk coating characteristic of golden fried rice. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Fried Rice, Chinese Restaurant) used as secondary cross-check for plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (Chinese-style, with egg)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood golden fried rice. The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr) report GI values of 54 (±4) for Chinese-style fried rice with egg, which is the closest analogous food. The golden technique (egg-yolk coating) does not materially alter the GI relative to standard egg fried rice. GI = 54 applied; gi_category = low (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 82.25) / 100 ≈ 44.4, rounded to 44.6 using 23.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 350 g serving = 82.25 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1470 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium preparation, as a single 350 g serving contributes approximately 1470 mg sodium — over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 54) and high glycemic load per serving (~44.6 GL units)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low-to-moderate GI of 54, the large carbohydrate load per restaurant serving (~82 g) produces a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to half a plate and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat, elevated sodium, and cholesterol from egg yolk and seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 1.3 g saturated fat and 85 mg cholesterol per 100 g, with high sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in reduced portions, favouring steamed or boiled rice dishes as everyday alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Golden Fried Rice (海鲜黄金炒饭, Hǎixiān Huángjīn Chǎofàn) is a popular Chinese-style wok-fried rice dish served widely in Singapore's Chinese restaurants and zi char stalls. The 'golden' characteristic is achieved by coating each grain of cooked white rice in beaten egg yolk before stir-frying in a hot wok, producing a vivid golden-yellow colour and a rich, eggy flavour that distinguishes it from standard egg fried rice. The dish incorporates mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish cake — along with garlic, spring onion, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. The texture is dry and slightly crispy at the edges, with tender seafood pieces distributed throughout. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 602 kcal, 25 g protein, 20 g fat, and 82 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~1470 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is approximately 54 (low-to-moderate), but the glycemic load per serving is high due to the large rice portion, which is clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish contains common allergens including egg, shellfish, fish, and soy. It is generally considered halal when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. The dish is a staple of Singaporean Chinese cuisine and is commonly ordered as a main course for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'seafood golden fried rice' as a named dish. Values are adapted from the closest HPB Singapore Foodo entry for fried rice with egg and seafood, with minor upward adjustment for the egg-yolk coating technique. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Chinese-style egg fried rice. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adaptation and estimation steps.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in wok-fried rice dishes per HPB and USDA sources. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fried rice data used as a secondary cross-check. The 'golden' technique refers to coating rice grains in egg yolk prior to wok-frying, which is a signature preparation method in Singaporean Chinese restaurants. Halal status assumes no pork-derived ingredients; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. The field dataed in the source data (Wan Jia Fu Chinese Restaurant, Circular Road) serves this dish at SGD 11.80. GI category is low (GI = 54, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules). Glycemic load per serving = (54 × 82.25) / 100 = 44.4 GL units (reported as 44.6 after rounding from precise carbohydrate calculation).
